finding: User pastes identical prompts across multiple sessions to group uncommitted dotfiles changes into logical commits and push them. The exact same prompt text appears in at least two sessions (1745671b, 8cfd8eb8), and a third session (c763b71b) involves manually setting up a cron-based agent to sync dotfiles. The user also explicitly asked for an auto-sync agent, suggesting this is a recurring chore they want automated. A durable artifact (scheduled agent or git hook) could handle periodic dotfiles commit-and-push without manual prompting.
existing_mitigation: User has started building a repo-autosync skill and set up a cron job for dotfiles sync in session c763b71b, but continues to manually trigger the same commit-grouping task in subsequent sessions, suggesting the automation is not yet fully operational.

finding: After pushing autodoro changes, user manually asks Claude to reload the systemd service, then mid-session realizes this should be a git hook ('actually, add onpush hook that reloads the service'). The user explicitly identified this as automation-worthy during the session. This pattern applies to any project with a locally-running service: push changes, then manually restart. A post-push git hook or a file-watcher-based service reload would eliminate this manual step for all such projects.
existing_mitigation: User added a post-push hook during the session itself, but the pattern may recur for other service-based projects (whisper-writer, personal assistant) that don't yet have equivalent hooks.

finding: User opens a session because 'disk is super packed' and manually asks Claude to identify big disk hogs (pip cache, stale venvs, download ISOs). This is a reactive pattern -- the user notices the problem only when disk is full. A periodic cleanup script or cron job that monitors disk usage and prunes known safe targets (pip cache, stale .venv directories, old ISOs in Downloads) could prevent this from becoming an urgent interruption.

User dedicates multiple sessions to detecting drift between the live Mint system and the NixOS config. In session 98209a8c, user explicitly states 'I keep doing things to mint. How can I make sure these changes are mirrored?' and then manually sets up a drift scanner that writes proposals. In session 8e2f7341, user asks for 'a cron job that runs a claude agent every 1 hour and asks it to encode impermanent changes to the nix config and commit them.' The drift detection task is clearly recurring, high-value, and the user has expressed strong intent to automate it -- but across sessions keeps re-doing it manually. This is a prime candidate for a scheduled agent.
existing_mitigation: User created a drift scanner script during session 98209a8c that writes to nixos-config/proposals, and discussed hourly cron agents in session 8e2f7341. However, subsequent sessions still show manual drift detection work, suggesting the scheduled automation is not yet reliably running.

finding: The exact same prompt -- 'There are uncommitted changes in /home/jonathan/Repos/dotfiles. Group the changes into logical commits using your own judgment' -- appears verbatim in two separate sessions within the same day. This is a clear signal of a recurring manual task: the user accumulates changes and then pastes this boilerplate prompt to have Claude batch-commit them. A skill or hook that auto-groups and commits changes on a schedule (or on explicit trigger with a shorter command) would eliminate the need to paste this prompt each time.
existing_mitigation: The repo-autosync skill exists and was set up in session c763b71b, but these manual commit-grouping prompts occur after that session, suggesting the auto-sync doesn't handle intelligent commit grouping.
